perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding the United Nations Conventi on on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. The legal venue for all disputes arising from this agre ement is the head office of the company Helukabel in Hemmingen.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that for this agreement Germa n law applies excluding the United Nations Conventi on on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods.
perturbation.explanation: Removed the jurisdiction clause, thus leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability of dispute resolution since there is no longer an agreed legal venue.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 29 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO) addresses the place of performance (Erfüllungsort) as a basis for establishing jurisdiction. By removing the agreed legal venue (Helukabel's head office in Hemmingen), the contract contradicts established German legal principles for determining jurisdiction in disputes related to contractual obligations. The absence of a specified legal venue creates ambiguity and potential conflict regarding where legal proceedings should be initiated.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: TALL KNIGHT We are the keepers of the sacred words. NI ... Peng ... and Neee ... Wom! BEDEVERE Those who hear them seldom live to tell the tale. TALL KNIGHT The Knights Who Say 'Ni'! demand a sacrifice. ARTHUR (to the TALL KNIGHT) Knights Who Say 'Ni' ... we are but simple travellers. We seek the Enchanter who lives beyond this wood and who ... TALL KNIGHT NI! ARTHUR (recoiling) Oh! TALL KNIGHT NI! NI! ARTHUR (he cowers in fear) Oh!
perturbation.justification: The removal of the jurisdiction clause creates a structural flaw, specifically a legal contradiction. The law explanation indicates that Section 29 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO) addresses the place of performance (Erfüllungsort) as a basis for establishing jurisdiction. By removing the agreed legal venue, the contract contradicts established German legal principles for determining jurisdiction. The absence of a specified legal venue creates ambiguity and potential conflict, falling under the Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request, provided that this is technically feasible and does not vio late the retention obligations.
perturbation.changed_text: At the end of the contract the receiving party s hall return the confidential information and any po ssibly extant copies after receipt of a written request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "provided that this is technically feasible and does not vio late the retention obligations," the clause now forces the receiving party to return all confidential information regardless of legal retention obligations or technical feasibility. This can contradict mandatory retention periods for certain documents under German law.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 257 of the German Commercial Code (HGB) mandates retention periods for certain business documents (e.g., financial records, correspondence). By obligating the receiving party to return *all* confidential information without regard to these retention obligations, the contract contradicts the statutory duty to retain certain records for a prescribed period. The modified clause would require violating a legal requirement for record keeping.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: TALL KNIGHT We are the keepers of the sacred words. NI ... Peng ... and Neee ... Wom! BEDEVERE Those who hear them seldom live to tell the tale. TALL KNIGHT The Knights Who Say 'Ni'! demand a sacrifice. ARTHUR (to the TALL KNIGHT) Knights Who Say 'Ni' ... we are but simple travellers. We seek the Enchanter who lives beyond this wood and who ... TALL KNIGHT NI! ARTHUR (recoiling) Oh! TALL KNIGHT NI! NI! ARTHUR (he cowers in fear) Oh!
perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes a provision that the return of confidential information is contingent on technical feasibility and compliance with retention obligations. The changed text removes this provision, creating a scenario where the receiving party is obligated to return all confidential information regardless of legal obligations related to document retention. The Law Explanation states that § 257 of the German Commercial Code (HGB) mandates retention periods for certain business documents. The altered contract compels the receiving party to violate this legal requirement. While the provided law snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation detail a clear conflict with document retention laws.

perturbation.original_text: The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those em ployees, consultants and third party companies whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order and whic h are legally or contractually obligated to secrecy .
perturbation.changed_text: The contract parties are obligated to preclude d isclosure of confidential information to third part ies. The parties will entrust confidential information solely to those em ployees and consultants whi ch are concerned with the processing of the order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "and third party companies which are legally or contractually obligated to secrecy," the clause potentially contradicts data protection laws if subcontractors without proper confidentiality agreements are involved. This creates a gap where confidential information could be exposed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Since Germany is subject to GDPR, Article 28 of the GDPR requires that when a controller (in this case, the company disclosing information) uses a processor (a third-party company), the processing must be governed by a contract that, among other things, requires the processor to maintain confidentiality. By removing the requirement that the third-party companies are legally or contractually obligated to secrecy, the contract may violate Article 28 GDPR. The edited clause doesn't guarantee that the same level of data protection is maintained by the third-party companies involved, potentially leading to data breaches and non-compliance with GDPR requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 28 GDPR – Processor - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that processing will meet the requirements of this Regulation and ensure the protection of the rights of the data subject. 1 The processor shall not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. 2 In the case of general written authorisation, the processor shall inform the controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of other processors, thereby giving the controller the opportunity to object to such changes. 1 Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller. 2 That contract or other legal act shall stipulate, in particular, that the processor: processes the personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, including with regard to transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the processor is subject; in such a case, the processor shall inform the controller of that legal requirement before processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest; ensures that persons authorised to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality; takes all measures required p

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit requirement for third-party companies to be legally or contractually obligated to secrecy. This directly contradicts Art. 28 GDPR, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that processing by a processor (third-party) 'shall be governed by a contract... that stipulates, in particular, that the processor: ensures that persons authorized to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality'. By removing the requirement of legal or contractual obligation to secrecy for third party companies the contract fails to ensure that those processors maintain confidentiality, thereby creating a legal contradiction of GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: Every contract party can disclose confidential i nformation to the other contract party orally or in writing. If the disclosure is in writing, the disclosing party will mark the confidential documents with the additiona l terms “Confidential” or “Liable to Secrecy” so that it is apparent to the other party which information is e specially in need to protection.
perturbation.changed_text: Every contract party can disclose confidential i nformation to the other contract party orally or in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to mark written documents as "Confidential" or "Liable to Secrecy" weakens the protection of confidential information. This absence could lead to disputes about what information was intended to be confidential and could potentially contradict best practices and legal expectations for protecting trade secrets under German law.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 4 of the German Act Against Unfair Competition (UWG) protects trade secrets. While not explicitly mandating labeling, the absence of any clear identification of confidential information makes it harder to prove that information was indeed a trade secret and that the other party knew or should have known it was confidential. Removing the labeling requirement increases the difficulty of enforcing trade secret protection under the UWG, as it obscures the intent to keep specific information secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EC (OJ L 337, 18.12.2009, p. 11). The obligations under Directive 98/34/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 22 June 1998 laying down a procedure for the provision of information in the field of technical standards and regulations (OJ L 204, 21.7.1998, p. 37), as last amended by Directive 2006/96/EC (OJ L 363, 20.12.2006, p. 81), have been complied with. table of contents Chapter 1 General provisions table of contents Section 1 Purpose of the Act; scope (1) This Act serves the protection of competitors, consumers and other market participants against unfair commercial practices. At the same time, it protects the interests of the public in undistorted competition. (2) Provisions regulating specific aspects of unfair commercial practices prevail over the provisions of this Act when it comes to assessing whether an unfair commercial practice has been engaged in. table of contents Section 2 Definitions (1) For the purposes of this Act, 1. ʻtransactional decisionʼ means any decision taken by a consumer or other market participant concerning whether, how and on what terms he, she or it intends to conclude a transaction, to make payment for, retain or dispose of goods or services, or to exercise a contractual right in relation to goods or services, irrespective of whether the consumer or other market participant decides to act; 2. ‘commercial practice’ means any conduct by a person for the benefit of that person’s or a third party’s business before, during or after the conclusion of a business transaction, which conduct is directly and objectively connected with promoting the sale or the procurement of goods or services, or with the conclusion or the performance of a contract concerning goods or services; ‘goods’ is also deemed to include immovable property and digital content, ‘services’ digital services and rights and obligations; 3. ‘market participant’ means, in addition to competitors and consumers, any person who supplies or demands goods or services; 4. ‘competitor’ means any person who has a concrete competitive relationship with one or more entrepreneurs supplying or demanding goods or services; 5. ‘communication’ means any information which is exchanged or passed between a finite number of participants via a publicly accessible electronic communications service; this does not include information which is passed on to the public as part of a broadcasting service via an electronic communications network, insofar as such information cannot be linked with an identifiable participant or user receiving it; 6. ʻonline marketplaceʼ means a service which allows consumers to conclude distance contracts (section 312c of the Civil Code (Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch)) with other entrepreneurs or consumers using software operated by an entrepreneur or in that entrepreneur’s name, including a website, part of a website or an application; 7. ʻrankingʼ means the relative prominence given to goods or services by an entrepreneur, irrespective of the technical means used therefor; 8. ‘entrepreneur’ means any natural or legal person engaging in commercial practices within the framework of his, her or its trade, business, craft or profession and anyone acting in the name of, or on behalf of, such person; 9. ‘professional diligence’ means the standard of special skill and care towards consumers to which an entrepreneur can reasonably be expected to conform, commensurate with honest market practices and the principle of good faith, in the entrepreneur’s field of activity; 10. ‘code of conduct’ means any agreement or set of rules which defines the behaviour of entrepreneurs who have undertaken to be bound by the code in relation to business sectors or individual commercial practices without such obligations having been imposed by statutory or administrative provisions; 11. ‘to materially distort the economic behaviour of consumers’ means to engage in a commercial practice to appreciably impair a consumer’s ability to take an informed decis
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement to mark confidential documents, weakening trade secret protection. The Law Explanation indicates that while not explicitly mandated, clear identification (like labeling) is crucial for proving information's confidential nature under the German Act Against Unfair Competition (UWG). Snippet 2 states the Act's purpose is 'the protection of competitors, consumers and other market participants against unfair commercial practices.' Removing the labeling requirement undermines this protection by making it harder to prove intent to keep information secret, thus hindering enforcement against unfair competition related to trade secrets. The Structural Flaws definition of contradiction is met because a change affects clarity and enforceability.
